meta.title: X1 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: BMW X1 • 2009–Present • 3 Generations (E84, F48, U11) • N47, N52, N20, N55, B38, B47, B48
hero.h1: X1 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW X1 engine across three generations — from the N47 diesel and N52 straight-six of the E84 to the B38 three-cylinder and B48 four-cylinder of the F48 and U11. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's entry-level SUV. Backed by real UK X1 engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The X1 rule — the platform divide: an N47 recon at £3,200–£5,500 against a £5,000–£8,000 E84 is 40–110% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ scrap. Against a £12,000–£20,000 F48, a B47/B48 recon at £2,800–£5,000 is 14–42% — ✅ safe. Generation decides everything.
★★★★☆ — the B38/B47/B48 are the most dependable; the N47 carries the chain-tensioner caveat; the N52 straight-six is robust but ageing
B48B20 (F48/U11) and B47D20A — the modular B-family engines are the modern gold standard
Pre-2015 N47D20C (E84) without documented timing-chain replacement
2016+ F48 sDrive 20i or xDrive 20d with B48/B47 — post-chain-revision, FWD-based UKL platform, sensible SUV packaging
F48/U11 sDrive 18i/20i with B38/B48 — the three and four-cylinder petrols are refined and reliable
🏆 The Entry-Level SUV — BMW's most affordable SUV and the gateway to X-family ownership

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The X1 is BMW's entry-level SUV — three generations, two platforms, and one clear dividing line: the E84's N47 chain question defines the early cars; the F48 and U11's B-family engines define the modern ones. Buy on the generation line, not the badge.

rows.metric: Sub-headline: Three generations, two platforms, and 15 years of BMW's entry-level SUV. The right X1 depends on which generation and which engine you choose.
BMW X1 E84 🔥 Most Enquired — N47 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]
rows.value: 2009–2015 • N46, N20, N47, N52, N55 — RWD-based (E90 platform)

Our Verdict: The original X1 — based on the E90 3 Series platform with rear-wheel-drive as standard. The N52 straight-six is the robust pick; the N47 diesel carries the chain-tensioner caveat; the N20 petrol has timing chain issues. Buy the N52 or a diesel with documented chain history.

rows.metric: BMW X1 F48 🔥 Most Enquired — B47/B48 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The second-generation X1 — switched to front-wheel-drive-based UKL2 platform. The B47 diesel replaced the N47, finally fixing the chain issue. The B38 three-cylinder and B48 four-cylinder are the modern gold standard. This is the X1 generation to buy.
2022–present • B38, B48, B47 — FWD-based (UKL2 platform), mild-hybrid, PHEV, iX1 electric

Our Verdict: The third-generation X1 — revealed June 2022, launched October 2022. Available with petrol, diesel, plug-in hybrid, and fully electric iX1 powertrains. The B-family engines are the most dependable ever fitted to the badge — buy with confidence.
The Platform Divide — The Line That Matters Most

rows.metric: Buy N52 or N47 with chain proof

rows.value: Read the full comparison: BMW X1 E84 vs F48 →

rows.metric: The Badge Ambiguity — "20d" Means Different Things
rows.value: Correction applied (internal): The "20d" badge covers two different engines depending on generation. On the E84 (2009–2015), it's the N47D20C — the chain-tensioner engine. On the F48 (2015–2022), it's the B47C20A/B — the revised architecture. On the U11 (2022+), it's also a B47 variant. All share a badge. None share a reliability profile.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW X1 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW X1 — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: N47D20C and B47D20A ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns. N52B30 and N55B30 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — well-documented across the E90/E84 era. E84 petrols (N46, N20): Pattern-data tier — documented by specialist consensus. B38/B48 ratings: Provisional — the engines are proven across the Mini and 1 Series ranges but X1-specific data is limited. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The F48 X1 (2015–2022) replaced the N47 with the B47 diesel. This is the single most important reliability improvement on the X1 — the chain issue was finally resolved. Correction applied: The E84 X1 (2009–2015) is based on the E90 3 Series platform — rear-wheel-drive as standard. This is a different driving character from the F48's FWD-based UKL2 platform. Correction applied: The E84 X1 offered the N52 straight-six (xDrive 28i) — a naturally-aspirated 3.0L six-cylinder producing 258 bhp. This was the robust petrol choice in the E84 range. The X1 35iX used the N55 turbocharged straight-six. Correction applied: The U11 X1 (2022–present) introduced the iX1 electric variant alongside petrol, diesel and plug-in hybrid options. This is the first electrified X1.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across the X1 range — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.description: The defining E84 X1 issue — the N47 diesel's rear-mounted timing chain stretches, causing cold-start rattle. Ignored, chain slip destroys the engine. Affects E84 18d/20d (2009–2015). £3,200–£5,500 recon [BMW-QUOTE].
The N20 petrol's timing chain guide failure — plastic guides break, chain slips. Affects E84 20i/28i (2009–2015). Rattling on cold start is the warning. £2,800–£4,800 recon.
The N47 diesel's EGR cooler can leak coolant, potentially causing fires. BMW issued recalls affecting 800,000 diesel engines. Affects E84 N47 diesels. Check recall history before buying.

The B47 diesel's primary weak points — EGR cooler leaks and DPF clogging on urban-driven cars. Affects F48 18d/20d (2015–2022). Well-documented, repairable, far less severe than the N47's chain issue.
The B48's known weak points — coolant expansion tank leaks, water pump failures, and PCV valve wear. Affects F48/U11 20i/25i. £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.
Not an engine failure — but a common non-engine X1 enquiry. The E84's xDrive transfer case can fail, causing driveline vibration and noise. Check on any high-mileage E84 xDrive.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW X1 Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — the B47/B48 cars are the most popular [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬆ Rising — new generation entering used market [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬆ Rising — the N47's reputation drives due-diligence searches

insights: Interesting insight: The X1 generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are SUV buyers doing due diligence on specific known issues (N47 chain, N20 chain) rather than performance enthusiasts. The X1 buyer is often a first-time BMW owner, making the N47/N20 chain question a critical decision point. The F48 with B47/B48 is the safe choice. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

The E84 X1 is now entering affordable territory — £3,500–£7,000 for a used example. But the N47 chain question means buyers need to budget accordingly: a £3,200–£5,500 recon on a £4,000 car is a hard decision. The N52 straight-six E84 is the robust petrol alternative — but they're rare. (Editorial synthesis.)

The F48 X1 with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol is the generation to buy — the chain risk is gone, the UKL platform is practical, and the values are sensible (£12,000–£20,000). The U11 is too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW X1 Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one X1 based on this page, buy a 2016+ F48 with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol — the B-family engines finally fixed the chain issue that defined the E84, and the UKL platform is practical and efficient. If the budget only stretches to an E84, buy the N52 straight-six petrol or insist on documented N47 chain replacement — a £4,000 E84 without chain paperwork is the trap, not the bargain.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW X1 Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW X1 engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The X1 rule — the platform divide: An N47 recon at £3,200–£5,500 against a £4,000–£6,000 E84 sits at 53–138% of the car's value — ⚠️ watch to ❌ scrap. Against a £12,000–£20,000 F48, a B47/B48 recon at £2,800–£5,000 is 14–42% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. The F48 with B47/B48 is the one to buy; the E84 N47 is the one to avoid without chain paperwork.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a BMW X1 engine typically takes 8–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The X1 rule — the platform divide: An N47 recon at £3,200–£5,500 against a £4,000–£6,000 E84 sits at 53–138% of the car's value — ⚠️ watch to ❌ scrap. Against a £12,000–£20,000 F48, a B47/B48 recon at £2,800–£5,000 is 14–42% — ✅ safe. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. The F48 with B47/B48 is the one to buy; the E84 N47 is the one to avoid without chain paperwork.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW X1 Engine Range Evolved — 2009 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW's entry-level SUV launched on the E90 3 Series platform — rear-wheel-drive as standard, sharing engines with the 1 and 3 Series.
eras.worthKnowing: The E84 is the only RWD X1 — it's the driver's X1. The N47 diesel carries the chain-tensioner caveat; the N52 straight-six is the robust petrol pick.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The switch to the UKL2 platform brought front-wheel-drive-based architecture and the B-family engines. The B47 finally fixed the chain issue that defined the N47.
eras.worthKnowing: The F48 is the X1 to buy — the B47/B48 engines are the most dependable in the range, the platform is practical, and the values are sensible.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The third-generation X1 brought the iX1 electric variant alongside petrol, diesel and plug-in hybrid options.
eras.worthKnowing: The iX1 is BMW's first electric X1. Too new for volume reliability data, but the B-family record is impeccable.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW X1?

profiles.verdict: The X1 is a practical family SUV — the F48 and U11 offer 505 litres of boot space and usable rear seats. The B47 diesel is the sensible choice; the B48 petrol is the refined option. Buy 2016+ F48 for the sweet spot.
The X1 is a great commuter — the F48 18d with B47 returns 55+ mpg, the UKL platform is comfortable, and the SUV driving position is popular. The U11 is even more refined.
A cheap E84 N47 is not a cheap X1 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £4,000 E84 with N52 or chain paperwork is the bargain; a £3,500 E84 without it is the trap.
The X1 isn't a performance SUV — but the xDrive 35iX (E84) with N55 is quick (300 bhp). The F48 xDrive 25i with B48 is the modern pick. Buy the X1 for practicality, not performance.
The X1 is BMW's gateway SUV — approachable costs, huge specialist coverage, and every mistake already documented. Start F48 or U11 and it's a genuinely easy ownership entry.

items.question: How much does a BMW X1 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned BMW X1 engine typically costs £2,800–£5,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the B38 at £2,200–£3,800 to the N47 at £3,200–£5,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which BMW X1 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The B48B20 petrol and B47C20A/B diesel in the F48 and U11 — the B-family engines are the modern gold standard. The N52B30 in the E84 is the robust classic pick.

items.question: Which BMW X1 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — but an E84 N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The N20 petrol in the E84 carries the same timing chain risk. An F48 B47/B48 has no such caveat.

items.question: How do I know if my X1 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: By engine: cold-start rattle (N47/N20 timing chain), chain noise under load (N47/N20), oil drips (N47 EGR cooler), coolant loss (B48 cooling system). On the E84, any rattling on startup is a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,800–£5,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a BMW X1 engine?
items.answer: Depends entirely on generation. Against a £12,000 F48, a £3,000–£5,000 B47/B48 recon is 25–42% — ✅ safe. Against a £4,000 E84, a £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon is 80–138% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

items.question: Which BMW X1 should I buy?
items.answer: 2016+ F48 with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol — the B-family engines fixed the chain issue, the UKL platform is practical, and the values are sensible (£12,000–£20,000). For the classic, E84 with the N52 straight-six — the only RWD X1 and the robust petrol pick. Avoid E84 N47s without documented chain history. The U11 is the future — buy it with confidence.

items.question: How do I find my BMW X1 engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (E84 18d, F48 20i, U11 18i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

items.question: BMW X1 vs X3 — which is more reliable?
items.answer: Mechanically they share engines across similar eras — the N47/B47/B48 story is similar. The X1 is smaller and lighter, so it works the powertrain less hard; the X3 is larger but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge. The X1 offers more value; the X3 offers more space. Read the full head-to-head →
